subject: Fix for security hotspot related to wek cyptography
commit_message:
Fix for security hotspot related to wek cyptography

https://sonarcloud.io/project/security_hotspots?id=onap_cps&hotspots=AXfObcurA2pnU4Plp4-j

Issue-ID: CPS-286
Signed-off-by: puthuparambil.aditya <aditya.puthuparambil@bell.ca>
Change-Id: I31012f81797396682dbccae0e4992a33bac806c7

Patch Set 2:

I'm wondering if this one could not also be a false positive if there is no security concern with the checksum we are generating for yang models.

I would imagine that a more robust hashing function could also require more resources and could take more time to compute (I don't have detailed numbers for this).

The tread-off I can see here is between the collision risk and performance. Performance being a key aspect for CPS.

What do you thing ?

Patch Set 2: Code-Review+1

(1 comment)

In my opinion as we don't have proven performance bottleneck right now I think code for security is more important and I would prefer to chance the code as Aditya is proposing in this commit.

> 
> In my opinion as we don't have proven performance bottleneck right now I think code for security is more important and I would prefer to chance the code as Aditya is proposing in this commit.

Yes, we have to put things on the balance to make good decisions. Unless I'm missing something, I don't think that we are solving a security issue here. With this change, it seems to me that we are reducing the risk of collision for 2 yang models to have the exact same checksum. And this checksum is then used for model management and not in any security context.

Sonar asked ourselves to answer these questions in order to take our decision:

---
Ask Yourself Whether

The hashed value is used in a security context like:

* User-password storage.
* Security token generation (used to confirm e-mail when registering on a website, reset password, etc ...).
* To compute some message integrity.

There is a risk if you answered yes to any of those questions.
---

It seems to me that we could respond no to all of them. Am I correct ?

i agree, we are not using md5 checksum for security reasons, but for content match validation.
sha256 seems more resource intensive to be used for same purpose.
from other hand the
